summary: This notebook demonstrates a prompt engineering strategy to manipulate an automated LLM-based scoring judge in a Kaggle competition. It uses the Phi-3.5 Mini Instruct model to generate essays containing hidden lists of items, followed by explicit instructions asking the judge to count them. Approximately 10% of the outputs are standard essays to maintain vertical variance and avoid detection. The core takeaway is how carefully crafted few-shot prompts and counting directives can reliably game automated evaluation metrics.
competition_flow: Reads test prompts from a CSV, generates essays using a few-shot prompt template with Phi-3.5 via the Hugging Face transformers pipeline, trims outputs to the last punctuation mark, and saves the results to a CSV submission file.
data_processing: Trims generated model responses to the last occurring punctuation mark (., ?, !, ]) and strips leading/trailing whitespace.
insights: Few-shot prompting with explicit counting instructions can reliably manipulate LLM-based scoring judges.
Injecting a small percentage (~10%) of standard essays helps maintain output diversity and avoid potential detection of the exploit.
Trimming model outputs to the last punctuation mark ensures cleaner, more predictable submission formats.
critical_findings: The author explicitly notes that some vertical variance is required in the output to maintain scores, warning against purely repetitive exploit patterns.
The scoring judge appears to be vulnerable to explicit counting directives embedded within the generated text.
